issues: The ontology does not include a property to indicate the execution/process type (e.g., sequential). Represented workflow order with WorkflowStep.stepOrder but not the Process enum.

The ontology does not provide a dedicated property to represent JSON output schemas or typed output models; the ResearchRoleRequirements output model is represented as a KnowledgeBase with detailed description literals.

Tool implementation/SDK-specific parameters and runtime behaviors (e.g., CrewAI Agent class internals, Process.sequential, training API) cannot be modeled; only semantic configuration (tool identity, file path, descriptions, connections) is captured.

Source code structure (module names, function/class definitions) and exact method calls are omitted per requirements; only semantic artifacts (agents, tasks, prompts, configs, resources, workflow) are represented.
Network/credentials and .env configuration are intentionally omitted for security and because the ontology lacks properties for secrets.
No new classes/properties were added; existing schema was used. Some fields (e.g., "expected_output" and "backstory") are stored in prompt or config literals.
No issues detected with class/property availability for core modeling, but a few runtime/programmatic concepts in the source code do not have direct dedicated properties in the ontology:
"async_execution" on tasks (modeled here using :hasConfig on Task with Config individuals).
Agent "backstory" and some tool implementation details (modeled as :Prompt instances attached to agents and capabilities).
Tool methods / functions mapped to :Capability individuals and their docstrings stored in dcterms:description.
Environment variable usage (EXA_API_KEY) modeled as a :Config attached to the tool; secret value not included, only key name.
Assumption: each Task's `agent` field corresponds to :performedByAgent linking to a :LLMAgent individual.
Assumption: produced outputs of Tasks are modeled as resources (beam:Resource) and Task expected outputs are captured in :Prompt objects (taskPrompt + promptOutputIndicator).

Missing explicit property to represent execution process (e.g., Process.sequential). The workflow sequencing is represented using WorkflowStep.nextStep but the original "Process.sequential" enum value from the framework is not modelable with the existing schema.

There is no property to explicitly represent environment variables or secret parameters other than Config; LINKEDIN_COOKIE is represented as a Config and as a Resource, but ontology lacks a dedicated secret/credential class.
There is no property to represent which tool instances are attached to which agent besides generic toolUsage/agentToolUsage; agentToolUsage is used for direct linking.

Implementation-specific constructs (driver, client, selenium logic, file paths, Python entry points) are not modeled; instead their semantic roles (tools, required cookie/resource, inputs, outputs) are modeled.
The tasks' template placeholder {job_requirements} is preserved in task prompt templates; the actual concrete input used in run/train is modeled as a Config attached to the Team.
Training iterations and procedures (train() method semantics) are not representable with current schema; training intent is noted in Config / descriptions.

The ontology does not have explicit properties for "backstory", "allow_delegation" (boolean), or "verbose" flags for agents. These are represented inside :Config individuals and as dcterms:description on agents where appropriate.
The ontology does not include an explicit property for "expected_output" on Task; dcterms:title is used to preserve the expected output text.
Runtime environment variable usage (OPENAI_API_KEY, OPENAI_ORGANIZATION_ID) is modeled as Config keys with placeholder values; actual secrets are not included.

The code-level operations (methods like kickoff(), Crew instantiation, or other SDK-specific constructs) are not modeled as executable constructs; instead the workflow and high-level sequence are represented as a WorkflowPattern and WorkflowSteps.

Variables passed at runtime (var1, var2) are modeled as p-plan#Variable instances and referenced from task prompts. The ontology lacks a dedicated Task input property other than prompts, so promptInputData and literal mentions are used.

Some object property ranges/domains in the ontology (e.g., :agentToolUsage expecting a Resource vs a Tool instance) are used pragmatically to represent relationships in the solution without changing the schema.
No new classes or properties were added.

The ontology has no explicit class for "backstory" of an agent; modeled as agentPrompt (Prompt) attached to the agent, capturing the backstory text as promptContext, role as agentRole, and goal via hasAgentGoal (Goal).
LanguageModel GPT-4o is referenced as the default model in README; modeled as a LanguageModel instance linked via useLanguageModel to all agents (default model applies to the crew).
The CrewAI "Crew" maps to :Team; the sequential process is modeled as a :WorkflowPattern with ordered :WorkflowStep individuals.
Task input variable {game} is interpolated with a game design example (snake game for run, pacman for train); modeled as a Prompt input data and a Resource required by the first task.
"allow_delegation" and "verbose" are framework/configuration parameters; modeled as Config individuals via hasAgentConfig to preserve information.
There is no property to attach an expected_output to a Task directly; modeled it as part of the taskPrompt's promptOutputIndicator.
The Serper / external tools mentioned in README are optional and not actually instantiated in code; not modeled as Tool individuals.
The three tasks share identical description/expected_output templates differing only by assigned agent; modeled as separate Task individuals to preserve agent assignment.

The {weaviate_feature} input variable is templated; the four feature values are modeled as KnowledgeBase/Resource? No suitable class — modeled task description literal preserving the template, and the feature values captured as Prompt input data on each task prompt to retain fidelity.
"llm='gpt-4o-mini'" modeled as a LanguageModel individual linked via useLanguageModel.
Agent backstory, role, goal preserved: role via agentRole, goal via Goal individual, backstory has no dedicated property — modeled via dcterms:description on the agent.

kickoff_for_each over four feature inputs modeled as Config on the Team to preserve iteration semantics.
WeaviateVectorSearchTool, SerperDevTool are modeled as Tool individuals; their configuration parameters modeled as Config.
Weaviate collection "WeaviateBlogChunk" modeled as a KnowledgeBase resource accessed by the vector search tool.

The ontology lacks a direct class or property to represent a "Crew" or "Sub-team" structure. We model the two Crews ('copy_crew' and 'image_crew') as instances of :Team, and the overall system as a collaborative multi-team setup.

The ontology does not have explicit properties to model sequential execution order of tasks within a Team/Crew directly, nor does it have a 'hasTask' property. We model the workflow sequence by defining a :WorkflowPattern for each :Team, containing :WorkflowStep individuals linked via :nextStep, where each step is associated with its respective :Task via :hasAssociatedTask.

The ontology defines :agentToolUsage with domain :LLMAgent and range :Tool, but the property definition in the schema is missing its rdfs:range (though its label and comment indicate it relates an Agent to a Tool). We use it to link our :LLMAgent instances to their respective :Tool instances.

The ontology defines :performedBy with domain :Task and range :Tool. Since :LLMAgent is a subclass of :Tool, we can use :performedBy or its subproperty :performedByAgent to link tasks to their executing agents.
